narration: In the second part of the QuickSort tutorial, the focus shifts from theory to practical C++ implementation, emphasizing the importance of the first video for understanding the algorithm's underlying logic.
The tutorial begins with creating a boilerplate C++ program structure to serve as the foundation for writing the sorting algorithm.
A size variable is created to determine the number of elements in the array, which is dynamically taken from user input using standard C++ syntax.
A for loop iterates through the array to input each element's value into the user-defined array.
Before the sorting process, the array elements are printed in their initial unsorted state to show the input data.
The quicksort function is defined to take the array, the start index, and the end index as parameters.
The main function calls the quicksort function by passing the array, starting index 0, and the ending index size minus 1.
The quicksort function checks if the start index is less than the end index to prevent infinite recursion.
The quicksort function calls the partition function to determine the pivot index and perform the in-place partitioning.
The partition function is selected as the last element of the array and stored in a variable named pivot.
A variable named P index tracks elements smaller than the pivot and is incremented during the comparison process.
A for loop iterates through the array elements (excluding the pivot) to compare each with the pivot value.
If an element is smaller than the pivot, it is swapped with the element at the current P index position.
The P index is incremented after each successful swap to mark the boundary for smaller elements.
After the loop, the pivot element is swapped with the element at the P index position to place it in its correct sorted location.
The partition function returns the final P index, which indicates where the pivot is placed and divides the array into two subarrays.
The quicksort function recursively calls itself for the left subarray (from start to P index minus 1) and the right subarray (from P index plus 1 to end).
The program is tested by entering a size of 5 and providing an unsorted array to verify the sorting functionality.
The quicksort function is tested with another set of input values to ensure the algorithm works correctly for various data sets.
